publications.title: BCLC Strategy for Prognosis Prediction and Treatment Recommendations: The 2026 Update
publications.authors: Reig M, Sanduzzi-Zamparelli M, Forner A, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: BCLC 2026.pdf
publications.results: BCLC 2026 update maintains direct linkage between disease stage and evidence-based first-line treatment while adding a clinical decision-making chapter using the CUSE framework: Complexity, Uncertainty, Subjectivity, and Emotion.
publications.findings: Key guideline update for HCC positioning. Useful for emphasizing stage-first-line linkage, multidisciplinary tumor board decision-making, and guideline-safe discussion of locoregional treatment selection.

publications.title: Effectiveness and safety of selective internal radiation therapy using yttrium-90 glass microspheres for hepatocellular carcinoma: real-world results from the multi-center prospective PROACTIF cohort of 989 patients
publications.authors: Guiu B, Bailly C, Vibert E, et al.; PROACTIF Investigators
publications.pdfFileName: 2026 Guiu PROACTIF.pdf
publications.results: Prospective multicenter real-world PROACTIF cohort of 989 patients with HCC treated with Y-90 glass microspheres.
publications.findings:
• Mean AD to the index lesion was 435·4 Gy, and increasing dose was associated with improved survival. mOS rose from 16·8 M for patients with a pretreatment AD to the index lesion <200 Gy to 26·0 M for those receiving ≥200 Gy.
• These findings are consistent with DOSISPHERE-01, which demonstrated a survival benefit for patients with a dose >205 Gy.8 Interestingly, patients treated with ≥400 Gy to the index lesion had substantially longer survival (median, 30·7 M) than those treated with <400 Gy (19·7 M).
• Further, the survival benefit was superior in patients with an index lesion >5 cm and index lesion dose ≥400 Gy (36·4 M) versus <400 Gy (18·5 M; p < 0·001).
• These data are consistent with a recent study evaluating Y90 glass microspheres in large tumors (≥5 cm) which identified a threshold of 586 Gy that more than doubled OS,6 further supporting an increase to the mean AD for large tumors to overcome the heterogeneity.
• Patients with segmental/sectorial PVT (Vp1/Vp2) had a mOS comparable to those without PVT, suggesting that personalized dosimetry-based treatment can at least partially mitigate the poor prognosis of Vp1-Vp2 PVT, although OS beyond the median still favored patie
publications.notes: Entered from PROACTIF ZIP. Full extraction should be manually checked before external use.

publications.title: Efficacy and safety of Y90 radioembolization combined with targeted therapy and immunotherapy in patients with unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma: a dual-center, retrospective study
publications.authors: Wang X, Jiang Q, Chen S, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2026 Wang Efficacy and safety of Y90 radioembolization combined with targeted therapy and immunotherapy in patients with unresectable HCC.pdf
publications.results: 32 uHCC patients; ORR 71.9% by mRECIST and 50.0% by RECIST 1.1. Median PFS 10.5 months and median OS 17.0 months. AFP >=400 ng/mL independently predicted worse PFS and OS; grade 3-4 TRAEs occurred in 43.8% with no treatment-related deaths.
publications.findings: Real-world data suggest triple-modality TARE plus targeted therapy and immunotherapy may provide encouraging antitumor activity with manageable safety in high-risk uHCC.
publications.notes: Accepted article in press; verify final publication details before external use.

publications.title: Feasibility of Radioembolization With Yttrium-90 Resin Microspheres Without Lung Shunt Fraction Measurement for Intrahepatic Cholangiocarcinoma
publications.authors: Lee M, Kim DH, Suh M, Paeng JC, Kim HC
publications.pdfFileName: 2026 Lee Feasibility of Radioembolization With Y90 Resin Microspheres Without LSF Measurement for ICC.pdf
publications.results: Single-center retrospective ICC cohort of 23 patients treated with resin microspheres without LSF assessment using defined eligibility criteria; median administered activity was 1.43 GBq.
publications.findings: Resin no-LSF workflow paper for ICC: supports feasibility of streamlined radioembolization without lung shunt measurement in carefully selected patients using institutional eligibility criteria.
publications.notes: Added from Resin ZIP. Review selection criteria carefully before any field use.

publications.title: Global landscape of yttrium-90 clinical trials: a systematic registry-based analysis
publications.authors: Ni Q, Wang K, Jin W, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2026 Ni Global landscape of y90 clinical trials a systematic registry based analysis.pdf
publications.results: 373 eligible Y90 trials from ClinicalTrials.gov (2000-2025); 86.9% interventional, 93.1% treatment-focused, 70.1% single-center, 54.2% enrolled <=30 patients, 68.4% North America-based, 11.5% Asia-based, and only 18.2% had published results.
publications.findings: Y90 trial activity has expanded but remains dominated by small, early-phase, single-center studies with uneven geography and limited publication of results.
publications.notes: Registry analysis; useful for research landscape and evidence-gap discussion.

publications.title: High-Dose Radioembolization with Resin Microspheres Limited by Lung Shunt for Localized Hepatocellular Carcinoma: Virtual Tumor Absorbed Dose as a Predictor of Complete Response
publications.authors: Kim HC, Lee M, Kim DH, Choi JW, Kim J, Suh M, Paeng JC
publications.journal: Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology
publications.pdfFileName: Large tumor/2026 Kim Resin Y-90 for Large Tumors.pdf
publications.results: Journal pre-proof evaluating high-dose resin radioembolization constrained by lung shunt, with virtual tumor absorbed dose assessed as a predictor of complete response.

publications.title: Impact of Genetic Mutations on Response and Time to Progression After Radioembolization of Breast Cancer Liver Metastases
publications.authors: Daana M, et al.
publications.journal: Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology
publications.pdfFileName: metastases/Breast cancer/2026 Daana Impact of Genetic Mutations on Response and Time to Progression After Radioembolization of Breast Cancer Liver Metastases.pdf
publications.results: Retrospective genomic profiling study evaluating whether mutation status was associated with response and time to progression after Y90 radioembolization for breast cancer liver metastases.
publications.findings:
• Overall median survival was 32.8 months (1.3 - 173.0).
• Patients with the ERBB2 mutation showed the longest median survival (70.2 months [12.5 - 173.0]), while the RAD21 mutation had the shortest median survival (25.5 months [2.4 - 173.0]).
• KDM5C and CBFB mutations were associated with the highest response rates (100%, p=0.003 and p=0.014, respectively), while the H3C13 mutation was associated with the lowest response rate (0%, p=0.002).
• Median time to progression was 32.8 months (1.4 - 173.0) in patients with an initial treatment response.
• The H3F3B mutation was associated with the longest time to progression (105 months [50.2 - 105.0]), while the RUNX1 mutation was associated with the shortest time to progression (1.4 months [1.4 - 36.5]).
publications.notes: Added from metastases ZIP. Source file: metastases/Breast cancer/2026 Daana Impact of Genetic Mutations on Response and Time to Progression After Radioembolization of Breast Cancer Liver Metastases.pdf

publications.title: Meta-analysis of efficacy and safety of Yttrium-90 radioembolization (TARE) in the treatment of advanced hepatocellular carcinoma
publications.authors: Lin H, Tan Q.
publications.pdfFileName: 2026 Lin Meta analysis of efficacy and safety of Yttrium90 radioembolization in the treatment of advanced HCC.pdf
publications.results: 40 studies including >10,000 patients; TARE improved OS (pooled HR 0.72, 95% CI 0.63-0.83) and PFS (pooled HR 0.78, 95% CI 0.69-0.89) versus controls; grade >=3 adverse events were lower with TARE (pooled OR 0.74, 95% CI 0.59-0.93).
publications.findings: Meta-analysis supports TARE as an effective and generally tolerable option in advanced HCC, with survival and safety benefits versus conventional/systemic controls.
publications.notes: Systematic review/meta-analysis; check included-control heterogeneity before making strong comparative claims.

publications.title: Post-TARE Survival Outcomes in Hepatocellular Carcinoma: Influence of Pre-TARE Immunotherapy and Targeted Therapy in a Real-World Cohort
publications.authors: Liu XY, Yu YX, Hong JH, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2026 Liu Post TARE Survival Outcomes in HCC Influence of Pre TARE Immunotherapy and Targeted Therapy in a Real World Cohort.pdf
publications.results: 71 HCC patients; patients receiving TARE as initial major treatment had higher ORR than those with >=2 cycles of immunotherapy plus targeted therapy before TARE (87.5% vs 56.4%, p=0.004) and longer median PFS (17.45 vs 4.86 months; HR 0.327, p=0.023). OS was not significantly different.
publications.findings: Baseline treatment history and inflammatory status appear to influence post-TARE response/PFS; findings do not prove superiority of a specific sequence.
publications.notes: Real-world cohort; sequence interpretation requires caution.

publications.title: Radioembolization of colorectal liver metastases: comparative dose–response of resin and glass microspheres
publications.authors: Soydal C, Mesci I, Celebioglu EC, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2026 Soydal Radioembolization of colorectal liver metastases comparative dose response of resin and glass microspheres.pdf
publications.results: Retrospective CRCLM study of 56 patients and 234 lesions using post-therapy Y90 PET/CT dosimetry. Glass n=30 and resin n=26; dose-response correlation was observed in both groups. Mean OS was not significantly different between glass and resin groups.
publications.findings: Strong support for a dose-response framing in CRCLM: response was linked to absorbed tumor dose, while product comparison should be interpreted through delivered dose and distribution rather than product name alone.
publications.notes: Added from Comparative / Dose Outcomes - Glass & Resin ZIP folder.

publications.title: Radioprotectant-loaded visualizable cationic radioactive microspheres: Reduced hepatic tissue damage and intra/postoperative imaging during TARE
publications.authors: Su XZ, Qiao EQ, Wu WY, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2026 Su Radioprotectant loaded visualizable cationic radioactive microspheres Reduced hepatic tissue damage and postoperative imaging during TARE.pdf
publications.results: Preclinical materials study developing visualizable cationic microspheres capable of loading iodine-131 and amifostine, with CT/DSA visualization and local radioprotectant release intended to reduce ROS-mediated liver injury.
publications.findings:
• In this study, microspheres (PAMS) were prepared by crosslinking polyacrylic acid hydrochloride and para aminophenol with epichlorohydrin.
• Subsequently, we grafted a large amount of iodine onto the microspheres through reaction with NaI, significantly increasing their density.
• This process led to the development of a cationic visual cationic quaternary ammonium salt based drug-eluting microsphere (PAIMS) capable of loading 131I and radiation protective agent amifostine.
• This type of microsphere not only provides sustained radiation therapy to damage the tumor after TARE treatment, but also continuously releases amifostine locally within the tumor to neutralize ROS in normal liver tissue, thereby protecting the liver without compromising the efficacy of TARE treatment.
• In vitro evaluation confirmed that PAIMS has good spherical and mechanical properties, meeting the performance standards required for embolic microspheres.
publications.notes: Preclinical technology paper; not a commercial Y90 microsphere clinical study.

publications.title: The predictive value of 18F-FAPI PET/CT and voxel-based tumor absorbed dose for the response and clinical outcome of unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma patients treated with Yttrium-90 resin microsphere selective internal radiation therapy
publications.authors: Gong H, Cheng Y, Li Q, et al.
publications.journal: European Journal of Nuclear Medicine and Molecular Imaging
publications.pdfFileName: 2026 Gong The predictive value PET:CT and voxel-based tumor absorbed dose for the response and clinical outcome of unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma patients treated with y90 resin microsphere SIRT.pdf
publications.results: 43 uHCC patients; 3-month target lesion response rate 60.5% and disease control 88.4%. Higher FTV-target/TLF-target predicted short-term response; lower Dmean <=151 Gy or D50 <=142 Gy independently predicted worse PFS.
publications.findings: Pre-SIRT FAPI-avid tumor burden and post-SIRT voxel-based tumor absorbed dose may help predict response and PFS after Y90 resin SIRT.
publications.notes: Prospective imaging/dosimetry study; product-specific to resin microspheres.

publications.title: Transarterial Radioembolization: Medical Staff Dosimetry for Y-90 Resin, Y-90 Glass, and Ho-166 PLLA Microspheres
publications.authors: Kühnel C, Schmidt TN, Freesmeyer M, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2026 k#U00fchnel Transarterial Radioembolization Medical Staff Dosimetry for 90YResin Glass 166Ho PLLA Microspheres.pdf
publications.results: Prospective single-center real-world staff dosimetry study comparing Y90 resin, Y90 glass, and Ho-166 PLLA workflows across multiple staff roles and procedure steps.
publications.findings: Supports the message that staff exposure is generally low and workflow/handling time matters. Glass workflow may reduce handling complexity because of ready-to-use design, but claims should stay tied to the measured workflow context.
publications.notes: Added from Glass vs Resin ZIP. Ho-166 included in paper but product filter kept as both because database product options are resin/glass/both/ns.

publications.title: Yttrium-90 radioembolization as an in vivo immune modulator: clinical evidence and pharmacologic implications
publications.authors: Tang S, Cai J, Wu P, Wang G.
publications.pdfFileName: 2026 Tang Y90 radioembolization as an in vivo immune modulator clinical evidence and pharmacologic implications.pdf
publications.results: Perspective synthesizing in vivo human evidence that Y90 TARE can induce systemic and tumor-microenvironment immune changes, including transient immune activation, myeloid remodeling, adaptive perturbations, and checkpoint-pathway upregulation.
publications.findings: Y90 TARE may function as an immune modulator, supporting rationale for combination strategies with immune checkpoint inhibitors while recognizing adaptive resistance mechanisms.
publications.notes: Perspective/review; mechanistic and hypothesis-generating rather than definitive clinical efficacy data.

publications.title: A novel triple-modality approach overcoming prohibitive hepatopulmonary shunt (HPS): Enabling yttrium-90 selective internal radiation therapy (Y90-SIRT) and subsequent curative resection in massive hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)
publications.authors: Luo Y, Lin Y.
publications.pdfFileName: 2025 Luo A novel triple modality approach overcoming prohibitive hepatopulmonary shunt Enabling Y90SIRT and subsequent curative resection in massive HCC.pdf
publications.results: Case report: initial hepatopulmonary shunt 21.8% reduced to 7.4% after arterioportal fistula embolization, DEB-TACE, and systemic therapy; Y90-SIRT enabled subsequent R0 resection with no recurrence/complications during 6-month surveillance.
publications.findings: Sequential multimodal management may overcome high lung shunt contraindication and enable curative-intent Y90-SIRT plus resection in selected massive HCC cases.
publications.notes: Case report; details should be checked before use in external materials.

publications.title: Baseline Hemoglobin Predicts Oncologic Outcomes in Intrahepatic Cholangiocarcinoma Treated With Yttrium-90 Radioembolisation: A Retrospective Cohort Study of 136 Patients
publications.authors: Abu Zahra S, Serhal M, Gupta AN, et al.

publications.pdfFileName: metastases/ICC/2025 Zahra Baseline Hemoglobin Predicts Oncologic Outcomes in Intrahepatic Cholangiocarcinoma.pdf
publications.results: Retrospective cohort of unresectable ICC treated with glass Y90; baseline hemoglobin was evaluated as a predictor of response, progression, duration of response, and overall survival.
publications.findings: HGB <10 g/dL appears to identify a higher-risk ICC subgroup with poorer oncologic outcomes after glass Y90 TARE.
publications.notes: Added from metastases ZIP. Source file: metastases/ICC/2025 Zahra Baseline Hemoglobin Predicts Oncologic Outcomes in Intrahepatic Cholangiocarcinoma.pdf

Clinical and dosimetric considerations for yttrium‑90 glass microspheres radioembolization of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, metastatic colorectal carcinoma, and metastatic neuroendocrine

publications.title: Clinical impact of Yttrium-90 resin microsphere radioembolization-induced hepatic adverse events and associations with predictive dosimetry
publications.authors: Fong JRE, Chen K, Goh CXY, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2025 Fong Clinical impact of Yttrium-90 resin microsphere radioembolization-induced hepatic adverse events and associations with predictive dosimetry.pdf
publications.results: Among 413 patients, 12 developed REILD and 17 developed grade 3 isolated hyperbilirubinemia; REILD cases were BCLC C with PVT and ALBI 2, and bilobar treatment was more common.

publications.title: Comparison of dosimetry models to predict outcome for radioembolization in patients with intermediate to advanced hepatocellular carcinoma
publications.authors: Liu HC, Liang PC, Yang YH, et al.
publications.journal: Journal of the Formosan Medical Association
publications.pdfFileName: 2025 Liu Comparison of dosimetry models to predict outcome for radioembolization in patients with intermediate to advanced HCC.pdf
publications.results: Retrospective Taiwan cohort of 89 intermediate-to-advanced HCC patients treated with Y90 resin microspheres, comparing BSA and partition model approaches and dose-outcome prediction.
publications.findings:
• The median overall survival (OS) was 15.8 months.
• The PM had significantly better OS than the BSA model (27.8 vs.
• The disease control rate in the seventh month was 40.9 % in PM and 31.1 % in BSA.
• Subgroup analysis for each dosimetry model showed improved OS in patients who received tumor doses over 125Gy (p = 0.040 for PM; p = 0.037 for BSA).
• CONC: lusion: Y-90 radioembolization using the PM and tumor volume are associated with OS in patients with intermediate to advanced HCC.
publications.notes: Added from Resin ZIP. Review final details before external use.

publications.title: Complications of Radioembolization: Prevention and Management
publications.authors: Kim GM
publications.journal: Journal of the Korean Society of Radiology
publications.pdfFileName: 2025 Kim Complications of Radioembolization Prevention and Management.pdf
publications.results: Review article covering common post-embolization symptoms and major adverse events such as REILD, radiation pneumonitis, and nontarget GI radioembolization, with prevention and management considerations.
publications.findings: Practical complications review emphasizing that radioembolization is generally safe, while serious complications can be mitigated through patient selection and careful preprocedural planning.
publications.notes: Added from Complications ZIP folder.

publications.title: Downstaging of hepatocellular carcinoma before liver transplantation: Results from a national multicenter prospective cohort study
publications.authors: Xu et al.
publications.pdfFileName: Resection/Curative Intent Downstaging and Bridging/Xu 2025 Downstaging of HCC multicenter cohort study.pdf
publications.results: Publication related to curative-intent downstaging or bridging strategy before resection/transplant; details should be manually verified against the full text.
publications.findings:
• There was also no significant difference in the proportion of patients who were foreign-born (27.4% in AC vs 27.6% in UNOS-DS;p=0.17) or spoke a primary language other than English (25.8% in AC vs 26.7% in UNOS-DS;p=0.21).
• There were no significant differences in liver disease etiology between AC and UNOS-DS (p=0.25), with the proportion of patients with Hepatitis C being the highest in both (46.3% vs 48.8%).
• A higher proportion of patients in AC had alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) ≥100 ng/ml prior to first LRT compared to UNOS-DS (35.8% vs 22.7%;p<0.01).
• A higher proportion of patients in AC received Y-90 versus TACE (50.0% vs 41.1%) as first LRT, compared to UNOS-DS which received more TACE than Y-90 (58.0% vs 33.7%;p<0.01).
• In the AC cohort, the probability of successful downstaging was significantly higher among patients who received Y-90 than those who received TACE (73.8% vs 64.6%;p<0.001) (Figure 2C).
publications.notes: Added from Resection ZIP. Source file: Resection/Curative Intent Downstaging and Bridging/Xu 2025 Downstaging of HCC multicenter cohort study.pdf

publications.title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma: ESMO Clinical Practice Guideline for Diagnosis, Treatment and Follow-up
publications.authors: Vogel A, Chan SL, Dawson LA, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: ESMO 2025 HCC.pdf

publications.findings: Guideline reference for aligning Y90/TARE discussion with broader HCC management pathways and multidisciplinary treatment selection.
publications.notes: Added from ESMO folder in Glass vs Resin ZIP. Review locally approved claims before slide use.

publications.title: High-Dose Radioembolization Limited by Lung Shunt for Hepatocellular Carcinoma Supplied by the Inferior Phrenic Artery
publications.authors: Kim HC, Kim DH, Suh M, Paeng JC, Choi JW
publications.journal: Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology
publications.pdfFileName: 2025 Kim High-Dose-Radioembolization-Limited-by-Lung-Shunt-.pdf
publications.results: Single-center retrospective study of 43 HCC patients treated with radioembolization via the inferior phrenic artery from 2017 to 2023. Mean posttreatment tumor absorbed dose was 629 Gy; best responses were CR 44.2%, PR 53.5%, and SD 2.3%. Two-year and five-year OS rates were 83.9% and 70.9%. Toxicity was generally acceptable, with one SIR grade 3 pain event and one SIR grade 1 symptomatic radiation pneumonitis.
publications.findings: High-value IPA paper for the dose discussion: lung-shunt limited high-dose radioembolization via IPA can deliver high tumor absorbed dose with strong local response, but lung shunt and pulmonary toxicity remain key planning constraints.
publications.notes: Added from Inferior phrenic artery ZIP. Review exact eligibility and lung-shunt workflow before external use.

publications.title: Histopathologic outcomes of hepatocellular carcinoma treated with transarterial radioembolization with yttrium-90 resin microspheres
publications.authors: Sarwar A, Nasser I, Weinstein JL, et al.
publications.journal: European Journal of Nuclear Medicine and Molecular Imaging
publications.pdfFileName: 2025 Sarwar Histopathologic outcomes of hepatocellular carcinoma treated with transarterial radioembolization with yttrium-90 resin microspheres.pdf
publications.results: 28 patients with 37 HCC tumors; complete pathologic necrosis in 27/37 tumors; all tumors with mean tumor absorbed dose ≥433 Gy achieved complete pathologic necrosis.
publications.findings: Important resin histopathology/dosimetry paper: pathologic necrosis correlated with post-treatment absorbed dose, supporting an ablative dose-response concept for HCC treated before surgery or transplant.
publications.notes: Added from Resin ZIP. Review before external use.

publications.title: Histopathologic Response and Oncologic Outcomes after Segmental and Subsegmental TACE and Radioembolization for HCC
publications.authors: Sher A, Todd RI, Shilo D, et al.
publications.journal: Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology
publications.pdfFileName: 2025 Sher Histopathologic response and oncologic out comes after TACE and Radioembolization for HCC.pdf
publications.results: Among 208 tumors in 148 transplanted patients, complete pathologic necrosis was 83.3% after TARE versus 29.0% after TACE; >90% necrosis occurred in 75.0% overall.
publications.findings: Strong explant-pathology comparative signal favoring segmental or subsegmental TARE over TACE for complete pathologic necrosis in HCC.
publications.notes: Added from Comparative / TACE ZIP folder.

publications.title: Perioperative and precision strategies in resectable intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma
publications.authors: Chen L, Han R, Song T, Song P, Tang W.
publications.pdfFileName: 2025 Chen Perioperative and precision strategies in resectable intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma.pdf
publications.results: Editorial review: discusses adjuvant capecitabine/S-1, neoadjuvant gemcitabine-platinum strategies, HAIC, Y90 TARE, molecular profiling, targeted therapy, and immunotherapy in resectable ICC.
publications.findings:
• Effective perioperative strategies are essential.
• Neoadjuvant therapy using gemcitabine-platinum combinations and locoregional strategies such as hepatic artery infusion chemotherapy (HAIC) and yttrium-90 radioembolization (Y-90 TARE) have improved resectability and survival outcomes.
• FGFR2 fusions, IDH1 mutations, and BRAF V600E mutations can be targeted with inhibitors such as pemigatinib, ivosidenib, and dabrafenib-trametinib, respectively, showing promising response rates in clinical trials.
• Immunotherapy has demonstrated efficacy in the microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) subtype.
• Combination strategies involving PD-1 inhibitors with radiotherapy or anti-angiogenic agents are further expanding the potential for treatment.
publications.notes: ICC perioperative strategy review; Y90 is discussed as part of broader multimodal care.

publications.title: Perioperative nursing coordination and complication management in Yttrium-90 radioactive microsphere therapy for hepatocellular carcinoma
publications.authors: Li X, Shang Q.
publications.pdfFileName: 2025 Li Perioperative nursing coordination and complication management in Yttrium-90 radioactive microsphere therapy for hepatocellular carcinoma.pdf
publications.results: 15 HCC patients; all completed Y90 resin microsphere treatment. Postoperative complication rate was 73.3% (11/15), mainly abdominal pain, fatigue, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and low-grade fever; no severe radiation-induced liver injury or significant myelosuppression occurred.
publications.findings: Structured perioperative nursing, radiation protection, vital-sign/lab monitoring, and tiered complication management may support safe Y90 microsphere therapy workflows.
publications.notes: Nursing workflow paper; center-specific experience with small sample size.

publications.title: Radiation Pneumonitis after Yttrium-90 Radioembolization: A Systematic Review
publications.authors: Kis B, Gyano M.
publications.journal: Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology
publications.pdfFileName: 2025 Kis Radiation Pneumonitis after Yttrium-90.pdf
publications.results: Systematic review compiling 48 reported radiation pneumonitis cases after Y-90 radioembolization. Mortality was 40%; lung dose was higher in glass-treated cases than resin-treated cases, while hepatopulmonary shunt fraction was not significantly different between groups.
publications.findings: Important cautionary review arguing that fixed lung dose thresholds may not be uniformly applicable across devices and that shortcomings in hepatopulmonary shunt calculation should be addressed.
publications.notes: Added from PVT ZIP / Radiation Pneumonitis folder.

publications.title: Radiation segmentectomy for early hepatocellular carcinoma is curative
publications.authors: Salem R, Padia SA, Toskich BB, Callahan JD, Fowers KD, Geller BS, Johnson GE, Kulik L, Patel TC, Lewandowski RJ, Kim E.
publications.pdfFileName: Radiation segmentectomy/2024 Salem Radiation Segmentectomy for Early Hepatocellular Carcinoma is Curative.pdf
publications.results: Expert opinion/review arguing that RADSEG should be recognized as curative for selected early HCC patients, citing ablative tumor dosing, complete pathologic necrosis at >400 Gy, durable time-to-progression, low local recurrence, and comparable outcomes to ablation or surgery in selected patients.
publications.findings: Strong positioning reference for curative-intent radiation segmentectomy in carefully selected very early/early HCC, especially non-ablatable tumors or challenging locations near critical structures.
publications.notes: Journal pre-proof; DOI 10.1016/j.jhep.2025.01.005. Added from Radiation segmentectomy ZIP.

publications.title: Radioembolization as a Spearhead Treatment of Hepatocellular Carcinoma with Localized Portal Vein Tumor Thrombosis (RESOLVE): Protocol for an Open-label, Multi-center, Single-arm Trial
publications.authors: Choi JW, Kim GM, Hyun D, Jang MJ, Kim HC.
publications.pdfFileName: Choi 2025 RESOLVE Radioembolization Y90 in PVTT.pdf
publications.results: Prospective open-label multicenter single-arm phase II protocol enrolling 30 patients with unilobar HCC and localized PVTT (Vp1–3), Child-Pugh A, ECOG ≤1, and no extrahepatic spread. Treatment includes radiation segmentectomy, modified radiation lobectomy, or ablative lobar treatment with glass microspheres.
publications.findings:
• To evaluate the efficacy, safety, and clinical course following ablative radioembolization with glass microspheres for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) with localized portal vein tumor thrombosis (PVTT) in patients with well-preserved liver function.
• Depending on the extent of the tumor and PVTT, patients will undergo radiation segmentectomy, modified radiation lobectomy, or ablative lobar treatment, while adhering to dose limits for the non-tumorous liver and lung.
• The primary outcome measure is overall survival, with the overall survival rate at two years provided as the summary measurement.
• STATISTICS: This study will enroll 30 patients to explore the efficacy and safety of ablative radioembolization for HCC with localized PVTT.
• Efficacy will be evaluated by intention-to-treat and per-protocol populations.
publications.notes: Added from PVT ZIP. Trial registration NCT06166576.

publications.title: Real-world outcomes of selective internal radiation therapy with 90Y resin microspheres in HCC/mCRC: RESIN registry Asia
publications.authors: Too CW, Liang PC, Chotipanich C, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2025 Too Real world outcomes of SIRT with 90Y resin microspheres in HCC mCRC RESIN registry Asia.pdf
publications.results: Real-world RESIN registry Asia report; response within 6 months occurred in 56% of primary liver cancer and 33% of metastatic tumors; median OS was 23.4 months and 13.6 months, respectively.
publications.findings:
• ORR at 3 or 6 months was 56% (HCC) and 33% (mCRC).
• Performance status was maintained or improved in most patients 6 months after SIRT.
• SIRT may offer clinicians a targeted, single-use approach for HCC or mCRC palliative care or for tumor down-sizing or down-staging in Asia.
• Controlled trials of SIRT using personalized dosimetry could further define a role for SIRT in these populations.
publications.notes: Added from Resin ZIP. Review before external use.

publications.title: Research trends of selective internal radiation therapy for liver cancer: a bibliometric analysis
publications.authors: Liu M, Xia T, Xu Q, Liu Y.
publications.pdfFileName: 2025 Liu Research trends of selective internal radiation therapy for liver cancer a bibliometric analysis.pdf
publications.results: 2,046 publications from 1994-May 2024 were analyzed. The US led publication output; co-citation analysis highlighted predictive dosimetry and atezolizumab; recent citation bursts included lung shunt fraction, tumor dose, systemic therapy, radiation segmentectomy, and Holmium-166.
publications.findings: Bibliometric mapping identifies personalized dosimetry, SIRT-immunotherapy combinations, radiation segmentectomy, and novel radioactive agents as major emerging research directions.
publications.notes: Research trend/bibliometric paper; not treatment efficacy data.

publications.title: Screening the advantageous population for liver cancer undergoing yttrium-90 microsphere selective internal radiation therapy
publications.authors: Liang L, Liang Y, Huang W, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2025 Liang Screening the advantageous population for liver cancer undergoing y90 microsphere SIRT.pdf
publications.results: Review summarizing favorable patient characteristics for liver cancer SIRT and key factors including tumor characteristics, liver function, performance status, mapping, lung shunting, catheter positioning, and dose planning.
publications.findings: Useful patient-selection review emphasizing mapping as a simulation step to optimize precision and minimize off-target risk.
publications.notes: Review article; use for selection framework and background.

publications.title: Transarterial Radioembolization in the TACOME Trial: Dosimetric Analysis and Clinical Features in Predicting Response and Overall Survival
publications.authors: Soydal et al.
publications.pdfFileName: metastases/mCRC/2025_Soydal_TACOME study mCRC.pdf
publications.results: Study or review related to Y90 radioembolization for mCRC liver metastases / non-HCC liver tumors. Details should be manually verified from the full text.
publications.findings:
• A strong correlation was found between mean ETAD and response to treatment (P = 0.001).
• The cutoff values to predict a response were calculated as 109 Gy (sensitivity, 68%; specificity, 73%; area under the curve, 0.728; P = 0.001) for mean estimated perfused volume absorbed dose and 152 Gy (sensitivity, 93%; specificity, 89%; area under the curve, 0.945; P = 0.001) for mean ETAD.
• The median overall survival (OS) of patients with a mean ETAD higher than 152 Gy was significantly longer than that of the remaining patients, at 18.1 mo (95% CI, 15.7-20.4 mo) versus 12.8 mo (95% CI, 10.6-15.0 mo; P = 0.030).
• Further analysis using maximally selected rank statistics to better predict OS showed that patients with a minimum mean ETAD of 203 Gy had OS 6.4 mo longer than the OS of those with a lower mean ETAD (P = 0.022).
• A mean radiation dose to the tumor of at least 152 Gy may predict a metabolic response.
publications.notes: Added from metastases ZIP. Source file: metastases/mCRC/2025_Soydal_TACOME study mCRC.pdf

publications.title: Streamlining Radioembolization without Lung Shunt Estimation versus Regular Radioembolization in Patients with Hepatocellular Carcinoma within the Milan Criteria

publications.journal: Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology
publications.pdfFileName: 2025 Kim StreamliningRadioembolization without Lung Shunt Estimation versus Regular Radioembolization in Patients with HCC within the MilanCriteria.pdf
publications.results: Compared streamlined TARE without nuclear medicine lung shunt estimation versus regular TARE in HCC within Milan criteria.
publications.findings: Supports selective streamlined workflow discussions for small HCC cases where lung shunt risk may be low, pending local practice and safety review.
publications.notes: Added from Lung Shunt ZIP folder.

publications.title: TACE vs. TARE for HCC ≥8 cm: A Propensity Score Analysis
publications.authors: Phan NH, Chun HJ, Oh JS, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2025 Phan TACE vs TARE for HCC 8 cm A propensity score analysis.pdf
publications.results: After PSM, OS was 33.2 months for TACE and 38.1 months for TARE; ORR, DCR, OS, and PFS were comparable, while TARE showed a safer profile.
publications.findings: Supports TARE as a reasonable initial option for unresectable HCC ≥8 cm, with comparable efficacy to TACE and potentially better safety.
publications.notes: Added from Comparative / TACE ZIP folder.

publications.title: Transarterial Radioembolization versus Atezolizumab-Bevacizumab for the Treatment of Hepatocellular Carcinoma with Portal Vein Tumor Thrombosis
publications.authors: Park Y, Cho Y, Kim SU, Kim A, Shin H, Kim HC, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2025 Park Y90 versus atezolizumab-bevacizumab for the treatment HCC PVTT.pdf
publications.results: Multicenter retrospective study of 213 treatment-naïve HCC patients with PVTT without extrahepatic metastasis treated with TARE (n=125) or atezolizumab/bevacizumab (n=88). Median OS was longer with TARE than atezo/bev (27.5 vs 8.6 months, P<0.01), including after IPTW and PSM adjustment. Benefit was observed in Vp1–3 PVTT but not clearly in Vp4. PFS and ORR were similar; adverse events were less frequent with TARE.
publications.findings: Very useful modern comparator paper for PVTT positioning: in selected patients with preserved liver function and segmental/lobar PVTT, TARE may compare favorably with atezolizumab/bevacizumab, but retrospective selection effects must be handled carefully.
publications.notes: Added from immunotherapy 2 folder inside Inferior phrenic artery ZIP. Article available online in 2025.

publications.title: Y-90 Selective Internal Radiation Therapy for Inoperable, Chemotherapy-Resistant Liver Metastases: A Meta-analysis
publications.authors: Hou S, Deng M, Hou Z, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2025 Hou Y90 Selective Internal Radiation Therapy for Inoperable Chemotherapy Resistant Liver Metastases A Meta analysis.pdf
publications.results: 28 studies / 1,662 patients; random-effects ORR 34%, disease control rate 64%, progressive disease 24%, any adverse events 57%, and grade 3-5 adverse events 20%.
publications.findings:
• Begg's test showed no significant evidence of publication bias.
• The random-effects weighted average overall response rate (complete response [CR] and partial response [PR]) was 34% (range: 26%-44%, I²=86%).
• The disease control rate (CR, PR, and stable disease [SD]) was 64% (range: 53%-75%, I²=91%).
• The rate of grade 3-5 adverse events was 20% (range: 7%-36%, I²=9%).
• This meta-analysis confirms that Yttrium-90 radioembolization is an effective treatment option for patients with liver metastases, demonstrating a high disease control rate with a relatively low incidence of severe adverse reactions.
publications.notes: Mixed tumor-type liver metastases meta-analysis; interpret heterogeneity carefully.

publications.title: Y90 Radiation Segmentectomy versus Microwave Ablation for Hepatocellular Carcinoma in Locations Suboptimal for Percutaneous Ablation

publications.findings: Y90 radiation segmentectomy was positioned as a valuable alternative to microwave ablation for early-stage HCC in locations suboptimal for percutaneous ablation, with favorable response and safety profile.
publications.notes: Added from Comparative / Ablation ZIP folder.

publications.results: Single-institution cohort of 48 Child-Pugh A patients with unilobar HCC and portal vein invasion treated with Y-90 glass microspheres. ORR was 83% by mRECIST and 88% by localized mRECIST; median OS was 47.2 months. Tumor absorbed dose was the only significant OS predictor, with >600 Gy proposed for improved outcomes.

publications.title: Yttrium-90 microsphere therapy for hepatocellular carcinoma: Clinical efficacy, mechanistic insights, and comparative therapeutic perspectives
publications.authors: Zhu YH, Wang MW, Jiao Y, Liu YH, Dong SS.
publications.pdfFileName: 2025 Zhu Yttrium-90 microsphere therapy for hepatocellular carcinoma.pdf
publications.results: Mini-review synthesizing clinical efficacy, mechanisms, patient selection, comparative advantages, dosimetry, and combination approaches for Y90 microsphere therapy in HCC.
publications.findings: Review highlights Y90 as a targeted treatment with favorable local control, manageable safety, dosimetry-dependent efficacy, and potential value in combinations with systemic therapy.
publications.notes: Mini-review; broad claims should be supported with primary studies when used in slide messaging.

publications.title: Yttrium-90 Radioembolization Has Become the Most Utilized Bridging Treatment for Liver Transplant Candidates in the United States
publications.authors: Salem R, Kwong AJ, Kim N, Sze DY, Mehta N.
publications.journal: Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology
publications.pdfFileName: Radiation segmentectomy/2024 Salem Yttrium-90 Radioembolization Has Become the Most Utilized Bridging Treatment for Liver Transplant Candidates in the United States.pdf
publications.results: Letter to the editor updating OPTN bridging/downstaging treatment trends. TARE was reported as the leading bridging modality in 2023 at approximately 35%, while TACE was second at approximately 30%.
publications.findings: Useful strategic reference showing contemporary U.S. adoption of TARE as a major bridging therapy for liver transplant candidates with HCC, with relevance to radiation segmentectomy and complete pathologic necrosis discussions.
publications.notes: Added from Radiation segmentectomy ZIP. DOI 10.1016/j.jvir.2024.10.034; published in JVIR Volume 36, February 2025.

publications.title: Applications of Yttrium-90 (90Y) in Hepatocellular Carcinoma
publications.authors: Jiang Z, Yang F, Wang W.
publications.pdfFileName: 2024 Jiang Applications of Yttrium90 in Hepatocellular Carcinoma.pdf
publications.results: Narrative review summarizing Y90 principle, advantages, indications, contraindications, efficacy, and adverse effects across HCC stages.
publications.findings:
• 90Y Selective internal irradiation therapy has become a localised treatment with good efficacy, safety and quality of life in the past decade.
• Since 2017,90Y selective internal irradiation therapy has been included in the primary liver cancer treatment guidelines; the Primary Liver Cancer Treatment Guidelines (2022 Edition) even states that:90Y selective internal irradiation therapy can be used for palliative (bridging or downstaging) treatment of liver cancer as well as explicitly stating that…
• Clinical trials to date have yielded promising results in early-stage patients, using tailored dosimetric and super-selective approaches that allow selective delivery of high doses of radiation to the tumor, yielding high radiological responses and long-term survival rates comparable to other options for therapeutic purposes (eg, surgery and ablation).
• Although 90Y has excellent efficacy in primary liver cancer, there is no clear evidence that 90Y has the same effect in other related cancers.
• For example, Intrahepatic Cholangiocarcinoma (ICC) is a primary liver tumour with a lower incidence than hepatocellular carcinoma.
publications.notes: General review; suitable for background reading rather than new original data.

publications.title: Arterial Hypoperfusion as a Negative Predictive Marker for Primary Hepatic Malignancies Treated with Y-90 Glass Microsphere Transarterial Radioembolization
publications.authors: Kalaghchi B, Ince S, Barnes J, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2024 Kalaghchi Arterial hypoperfusion as a negative predictive marker for primary hepatic malignancies treated with Y-90 glass microsphere transarterial radioembolization.pdf
publications.results: Retrospective analysis of 88 patients from 400 Y90 treatments who received prescribed dose ≥150 Gy and had pre- or post-treatment SPECT/CT images. Hypoperfused lesions were seen in 11 patients and hyperperfused lesions in 77 patients. Hypoperfusion was associated with shorter PFS (HR 1.87, p=0.039), higher elsewhere liver failure (HR 3.36, p=0.019), higher distant failure (HR 7.64, p<0.001), and worse OS in inverse probability weighted analysis (p=0.032).
publications.findings: Arterially hypoperfused primary liver tumors may respond less favorably to glass Y90 TARE than hyperperfused tumors. Useful for patient selection, expectation setting, and explaining why tumor perfusion and dose distribution matter beyond prescribed activity.
publications.notes: Added from hypo/hyper tumor ZIP. Review exact cohort definitions before external use.

publications.title: Comparison of 3 Different Therapeutic Particles in Radioembolization of Locally Advanced Intrahepatic Cholangiocarcinoma
publications.authors: Wagemans MEHM, Kunnen B, Stella M, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2024 Wagemans Comparison of 3 Different Therapeutic Particles in Radioembolization of Locally Advanced Intrahepatic Cholangiocarcinoma.pdf
publications.results: Compared three therapeutic particles used for radioembolization in locally advanced intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma.
publications.findings:
• Six 90Y-glass, 8 90Y-resin, and 7 166Ho microsphere patients were included for analysis.
• The mean administered activity was 2.6 GBq for 90Y-glass, 1.5 GBq for 90Y-resin, and 7.0 GBq for 166Ho microspheres.
• Tumor-absorbed dose and treated-normal-liver-absorbed dose were significantly higher for 90Y-glass than for 90Y-resin and 166Ho microspheres (mean tumor-absorbed dose, 197 Gy for 90Y-glass vs.
• 73 Gy for 90Y-resin and 50 Gy for 166Ho; mean treated-normal-liver-absorbed dose, 79 Gy for 90Y-glass vs.
• 37 Gy for 90Y-resin and 31 Gy for 166Ho).
publications.notes: Includes particle comparison beyond a simple glass-versus-resin framing.

publications.title: Complete Response to Locoregional Therapy Plus Immunotherapy for Hepatocellular Carcinoma
publications.authors: Chiang CL, Chan KSK, Chiu KWH, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2024 Chiang Complete Response to Locoregional Therapy Plus Immunotherapy for Hepatocellular Carcinoma.pdf
publications.results: Cohort study of 63 unresectable HCC patients treated with locoregional therapy plus immunotherapy in two prospective studies. Twenty-nine patients (46.0%) achieved radiologic complete response. CR patients had better 3-year OS than non-CR patients (75.5% vs 28.1%, P<0.001). Among CR patients, 3-year time-to-progression was 58.7% and 3-year local control was 90.5% after a watch-and-wait strategy and IO discontinuation.
publications.findings: Useful locoregional-plus-IO outcome paper showing that durable complete response is possible in locally advanced HCC, especially in patients with smaller tumor burden and no vascular invasion. This is LRT/IO context rather than a pure Y90 paper.
publications.notes: Added from immunotherapy 2 folder inside Inferior phrenic artery ZIP. Treatment exposure was SBRT followed by anti-PD-1/PD-L1 therapy, with many patients receiving TACE before SBRT; not specifically TARE.

publications.title: Efficacy and Safety of Radiation Segmentectomy with 90Y Resin Microspheres for Hepatocellular Carcinoma
publications.authors: Sarwar A, Malik MS, Vo NH, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2024 SARWAR Efficacy and Safety of Radiation Segmentectomy with 90Y Resin Microspheres for Hepatocellular Carcinoma.pdf
publications.results: Retrospective study of 67 HCC patients; median tumor absorbed dose 232 Gy; 6-month lesion-based response 94%; 2-year progression was lower with tumor absorbed dose ≥300 Gy.
publications.findings:
• Purpose To evaluate the efficacy and safety of RS treatment of HCC with 90Y resin microspheres using a single-compartment MIRD model and correlate posttreatment dose with outcomes.
• Materials and Methods This retrospective single-center study included adult patients with HCC who underwent RS with 90Y resin microspheres between July 2014 and December 2022.
• Per-lesion and overall response rates (ie, complete response [CR], objective response, disease control, and duration of response) were assessed at imaging using the Modified Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors, and overall survival (OS) was assessed using Kaplan-Meier analysis.
• Results Among 67 patients (median age, 69 years [IQR, 63-78 years]; 54 male patients) with HCC, median tumor absorbed dose was 232 Gy (IQR, 163-405 Gy).
• At 6 months (n = 46), per-lesion rates of objective response and disease control were both 94%, and per-patient rates were both 78%.
publications.notes: Added from Resin ZIP. Review before external use.

publications.title: EMERALD-Y90: A Phase 2 Study to Evaluate Transarterial Radioembolization Followed by Durvalumab and Bevacizumab for Unresectable HCC Eligible for Embolization

publications.title: Glass or Resin: The Ends Justify the Means?
publications.authors: Choi JW

publications.results: Commentary discussing the trade-off between high specific activity / lower particle number with glass and higher particle number / more embolic effect with resin, in the context of contemporary HCC comparative data.
publications.findings: Good conceptual piece for framing the debate: the question is not simply particle number versus dose, but whether each device is used appropriately for the treatment goal.

publications.title: Long-Term Overall Survival After Selective Internal Radiation Therapy for Locally Advanced Hepatocellular Carcinomas: Updated Analysis of DOSISPHERE-01 Trial
publications.authors: Garin E, Tselikas L, Guiu B, et al.

publications.title: Measurement of the Tumor-to-Normal Ratio for Radioembolization of Hepatocellular Carcinoma: A Prospective Study Comparing 2-Dimensional Perfusion Angiography, Technetium-99m Macroaggregated Albumin, and Yttrium-90 SPECT/CT
publications.authors: Brunson CP, McGregor HJ, Hennemeyer CT, Patel MV, Woodhead GJ, Young SJ.
publications.journal: Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology
publications.pdfFileName: TN ratio/2024 Brunson Measurement of the TN Ratio for Radioembolization of HCC.pdf
publications.results: Prospective single-arm study comparing T:N ratio estimates from 2D perfusion angiography, Tc-99m MAA SPECT/CT, and Y90 SPECT/CT in HCC radioembolization planning and assessment.
publications.findings:
• The mean participant age was 64.1 years ± 9.8, and the study included 14 (93%) men and 1 (7%) woman.
• The mean tumor size was 4.1 cm (SD ± 2.4), and all participants received segmental treatments with glass microspheres.
• The mean T:N ratio calculated by 99mTc MAA SPECT/CT was 2.28 (SD ± 0.89) vs 2.25 (SD ± 0.99) calculated by 2D perfusion angiography (P =.45).
• For the 13 participants who underwent selective internal radiation therapy (transarterial radioembolization), there was no significant difference between the T:N ratios calculated by 2D perfusion angiography and post-90Y SPECT/CT (2.25 [SD ± 1.05] vs 1.91 [SD ± 0.39]; P =.12).
• The T:N ratio calculated by 2D perfusion angiography correlated well with that calculated by 99mTc MAA SPECT/CT.
publications.notes: Added from ROWAN ZIP. DOI 10.1016/j.jvir.2023.09.023.

publications.title: Multicenter evaluation of the safety and efficacy of selective internal radiation therapy with yttrium-90 resin microspheres in Taiwan: data from the RESIN registry
publications.authors: Lee RC, Liang PC, Liang HL, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2024 LeeMulticenter evaluation of the safety and efficacy of selective internal radiation therapy with yttrium-90 resin microspheres in Taiwan data from th.pdf

publications.title: Phantom study for 90Y liver radioembolization dosimetry with a long axial field-of-view PET/CT
publications.authors: Mercolli L, Zeimpekis K, Prenosil GA, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: Simplicity/2024 Mercolli Phantom study for 90Y liver radioembolization dosimetry with a long axial field of view PET CT.pdf
publications.results: Phantom study comparing Monte Carlo dose calculation with commercial dosimetry software approaches using long axial field-of-view PET/CT for Y90 liver radioembolization.
publications.findings:
• The image-based dose calculation with Hermes, however, consistently underestimates the dose.
• We found that only for the smallest phantom sphere there is a statistically significant dependence of the Hermes dose on the image reconstruction parameters and scan time.
• Our study shows that Simplicit90Y's local deposition model can provide a reliable dose estimate.

publications.title: Predictive Dosimetry and Outcomes of Hepatocellular Carcinoma Treated by Yttrium-90 Resin Microsphere Radioembolization: A Retrospective Analysis Using Technetium-99m Macroaggregated Albumin SPECT/CT and Planning Software

publications.title: Stereotactic Body Radiotherapy and Liver Transplant for Liver Cancer
publications.authors: Lee et al.
publications.pdfFileName: Resection/Curative Intent Downstaging and Bridging/2024 Lee SBRT and liver transplant for liver cancer.pdf
publications.results: Publication related to curative-intent downstaging or bridging strategy before resection/transplant; details should be manually verified against the full text.
publications.findings:
• The median PFS was 17.6 months (95% CI, 6.6-28.6 months) with a 5-year PFS rate of 39.9% (95% CI, 19.9%-59.9%) (Figure 2A).
• The median OS was 60.5 months (95% CI, 29.7-91.2 months), with a 5-year OS rate of 51.3% (95% CI, 31.7%-70.9%) (Figure 2B).
• Patients with CMR in all SBRT-treated lesions had a longer median PFS (not reached; P =.03) and OS (60.5 months; P =.009) compared with those whose tumors did not attain CMR (median PFS, 10.0 months; median OS, 19.9 months) (eFigure 3 in Supplement 2).
• Patients whose lesions were within the Milan criteria had a trend of longer PFS (median, 27.0 vs 10.0 months; P =.08) and a significantly longer OS (median, 60.5 vs 19.9 months; P =.01) (eFigure 4 in Supplement 2).
• Those who received DDLT had a significantly longer PFS (median, not reached vs 10.0 months; P =.008) and OS (median, not reached vs 11.2 months; P <.001) (eFigure 5 in Supplement 2).
publications.notes: Added from Resection ZIP. Source file: Resection/Curative Intent Downstaging and Bridging/2024 Lee SBRT and liver transplant for liver cancer.pdf

publications.results: Retrospective comparison of stereotactic body radiotherapy and Y90 as ablative treatment options for localized HCC, including treatment outcomes and cost-related analysis.

publications.authors: Salem R, Garin E, Boucher E, et al.

publications.results: Additional EPOCH analyses evaluated TARE plus chemotherapy versus chemotherapy alone in mCRC liver metastases after first-line progression. Selected subgroups showed improved PFS, hepatic PFS, time to QoL deterioration, and time to subsequent therapy with TARE/Chemo.
publications.findings: Useful for patient-selection messaging in CRCLM: outcomes may be stronger when considering baseline ECOG, CEA, and KRAS status rather than treating the whole second-line population as uniform.

publications.title: Using Voxel-Based Dosimetry to Evaluate Sphere Concentration and Tumor Dose in HCC Treated with Y90 Radiation Segmentectomy with Glass Microspheres
publications.authors: Sandow T, Gimenez J, Nunez K, et al.
publications.journal: Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology
publications.pdfFileName: 2024 Sandow Using Voxel-based Dosimetry to evaluate sphere concentration and tumor dose.PDF
publications.results: Retrospective analysis of 56 solitary HCC tumors treated with glass Y90 radiation segmentectomy. Median tumor absorbed dose was 732 Gy; objective response was 96%, complete response was 82%, 2-year progression rate was 11%, and median tumor necrosis in transplanted patients was 99%.
publications.findings: Voxel-based post-treatment dosimetry can characterize sphere deposition and dose heterogeneity; higher D99 and lower tumor volume were associated with complete pathologic necrosis.
publications.notes: Added from Dosimetry ZIP.

publications.title: Yttrium-90 Radioembolization Dosimetry: Dose Considerations, Optimization, and Tips

publications.findings: Good educational reference for building a practical dosimetry training section because it explains how to think about dose selection and model choice without requiring the audience to become medical physicists overnight.
publications.notes: Added from Dosimetry ZIP.

publications.title: A Phase I/IIa Trial of Yttrium-90 Radioembolization in Combination with Durvalumab for Locally Advanced Unresectable Hepatocellular Carcinoma
publications.authors: Lee YB, Nam JY, Cho EJ, Lee JH, Yu SJ, Kim HC, Paeng JC, Yoon JH, Kim YJ
publications.pdfFileName: 2023 Lee Y90 + durvalumab for advanced HCC.pdf
publications.results: Single-arm phase I/IIa SOLID trial. Twenty-four patients received Y90 radioembolization and 23 received durvalumab. Median TTP was 15.2 months, median PFS 6.9 months, median OS was not reached, and the 18-month OS rate was 58.3%. ORR by mRECIST was 83.3%, including CR in 29.2% and PR in 54.2%. Grade 3 treatment-related adverse events occurred in 8.7%; no treatment-related serious adverse events were reported.
publications.findings: Strong combination-therapy signal for Y90 followed by durvalumab in locally advanced unresectable HCC, supporting the biologic and clinical rationale for pairing radioembolization with PD-L1 inhibition while still needing randomized validation.
publications.notes: Added from immunotherapy 2 folder inside Inferior phrenic artery ZIP.

publications.title: Bilobar Radioembolization Carries the Risk of Radioembolization-Induced Liver Disease in the Treatment of Advanced Hepatocellular Carcinoma: Safety and Efficacy Comparison to Systemic Therapy with Atezolizumab/Bevacizumab
publications.authors: Jeschke M, Ludwig JM, Leyh C, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2023 Jeschke safety of Y90 bilobar vs atezo bev.pdf
publications.results: Retrospective real-world cohort of 74 advanced HCC patients involving both liver lobes: bilobar TARE n=33 versus atezolizumab/bevacizumab n=41. OS numerically favored systemic therapy (13.0 vs 7.1 months, p=0.07). Similar disease control was observed, but OS in the TARE group was limited by REILD; baseline liver function predicted REILD in cirrhotic patients.
publications.findings: Important cautionary paper: whole-liver/bilobar TARE can carry meaningful liver-toxicity risk, so patient selection, baseline liver function, and treatment volume matter when comparing TARE with modern systemic therapy.
publications.notes: Added from Immunotherapy ZIP.

publications.title: Combining Selective Internal Radiation Therapy with Immunotherapy in Treating Hepatocellular Carcinoma and Hepatic Colorectal Metastases: A Systematic Review
publications.authors: Ramdhani K, Smits MLJ, Lam MGEH, Braat AJAT
publications.pdfFileName: 2023 Ramdhani SBRT plus immunotherapy.pdf
publications.results: Systematic review of SIRT plus immunotherapy in HCC and mCRC. Four early-phase patient studies with 87 total patients were included. No increased toxicity was observed versus known monotherapy data. The two mCRC studies showed limited response, while HCC studies reported median OS of 16.5 and 16.2 months, median PFS of 5.6 and 5.7 months, and ORR ranging from 31% to 80%.
publications.findings: Good summary paper for the early evidence base: SIRT plus immunotherapy appears feasible and safe, but clinical benefit is still uncertain and disease-specific, with stronger early signals in HCC than mCRC.
publications.notes: Added from immunotherapy 2 folder inside Inferior phrenic artery ZIP. Filename mentions SBRT, but article is about SIRT plus immunotherapy.

publications.title: Evolution of Personalized Dosimetry for Radioembolization of Hepatocellular Carcinoma
publications.authors: Knight GM, Gordon AC, Gates V, et al.
publications.journal: Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology
publications.pdfFileName: Knight GM et al 2023 NW Evolution  of personalized doismetry for HCC- Review Article.pdf
publications.results: Review article describing the evolution of Y90 TARE from palliative lobar therapy to personalized, goal-directed treatment across BCLC stages, including palliation, bridging/downstaging, conversion to resection, and ablative intent.
publications.findings: Strong narrative reference for why personalized dosimetry matters: treatment dose and distribution should be adapted to patient, lesion, and treatment intent rather than relying on one-size-fits-all activity planning.

publications.title: Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma: a dose threshold evaluation in those undergoing transarterial radioembolization
publications.authors: Young et al.
publications.pdfFileName: metastases/ICC/2023 Young ICC a dose threshold evaluation in those undergoing transarterial radioemboliza.pdf
publications.results: Study or review related to Y90 radioembolization for ICC liver metastases / non-HCC liver tumors. Details should be manually verified from the full text.
publications.findings:
• When looking at the likelihood of obtaining a complete response (CR) again no significant difference was seen between glass (8/17, 47.1%) and resin (1/9, 11.1%) (P=0.098).
• The mean tumor dose (MTD) for stable disease (SD) (1/17, 5.8%), partial response (PR) (8/17, 47.1%), and CR (8/17, 47.1%) by EASL was 294±0, 465.4±292.4 and 951.8±666.5 Gy respectively (P=0.039).
• Youden index analysis demonstrated a cutoff point of >103.2 Gy resulted in a sensitivity of 50% and a specificity of 100% for achieving an ORR.
• RR and relationship to dose: The mean tumor dose (MTD) for stable disease (SD) (1/17, 5.8%), partial response (PR) (8/17, 47.1%), and CR (8/17, 47.1%) by EASL was 294±0, 465.4±292.4 and 951.8±666.5 Gy respectively (P=0.039).
• Glass only: The mean tumor dose (MTD) for stable disease (SD) (1/17, 5.8%), partial response (PR) (8/17, 47.1%), and CR (8/17, 47.1%) by EASL was 294±0, 465.4±292.4 and 951.8±666.5 Gy respectively (P=0.039).

publications.title: Immunotherapy and Transarterial Radioembolization Combination Treatment for Advanced Hepatocellular Carcinoma
publications.authors: Yeo YH, Liang J, Lauzon M, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2023 Yeo Immunotherapy and TARE combination Tx for Adv HCC.pdf
publications.results: National Cancer Database study of 1,664 patients with advanced HCC diagnosed from 2017 to 2019. 142 received combined TARE plus immunotherapy and 1,522 received immunotherapy alone as first-line treatment. Median OS was higher with combination therapy than immunotherapy alone (19.8 vs 9.5 months); combined therapy was independently associated with reduced mortality (adjusted HR 0.50, 95% CI 0.36–0.68, P<0.001).
publications.findings: Large real-world comparative signal suggesting TARE plus immunotherapy may improve survival versus immunotherapy alone in advanced HCC, though selection bias and lack of regimen-level details should be considered.

publications.title: Locoregional Therapies for Hepatocellular Carcinoma prior to Liver Transplant: Comparative Pathologic Necrosis, Radiologic Response, and Recurrence
publications.authors: Mosenthal et al.
publications.journal: The Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology
publications.pdfFileName: Resection/Curative Intent Downstaging and Bridging/2023 Mosenthal M et al 2023 LRT of HCC prior to LT comparison of CPN, response and recurrence.pdf
publications.results: Publication related to curative-intent downstaging or bridging strategy before resection/transplant; details should be manually verified against the full text.
publications.findings:
• High dose 90Y radiation segmentectomy (≥190 Gy; n = 27) and TACE/ablation (n = 7) showed highest rates of complete pathologic necrosis (CPN)-63% (n = 17) and 71% (n = 5), respectively.
• Patients with CPN had a mean lesion size of 2.5 cm, compared with 3.2 cm without CPN (P =.04), irrespective of LRT modality.
• Radiation segmentectomy and multimodal therapy significantly improved CPN rates compared with TACE alone.
• A LI-RADS treatment response of nonviable did not confidently predict CPN.

publications.title: Multimodal Molecular Landscape of Response to Y90-Resin Microsphere Radioembolization Followed by Nivolumab for Advanced Hepatocellular Carcinoma
publications.authors: Kaya NA, Tai D, Lim X, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2023 Kaya N resin Y90 followed by nivolumab.pdf
publications.results: Translational analysis of 33 patients from a phase 2 trial of Y90 resin microspheres followed by nivolumab for advanced HCC. Prior trial response rate was 30.6%. Responders more often had higher tumor mutation burden, NCOR1 mutations, interferon-gamma pathway expression, and CXCL9+/CXCR3+ macrophage features; non-responders were enriched for an Asian-specific transcriptomic subtype with chromosome 16 deletions and cell-cycle pathway upregulation.
publications.findings: High-value biomarker paper for Y90 plus nivolumab: response biology may differ from conventional ICI biomarkers, supporting the need for combination-specific translational evidence and patient-selection strategies.
publications.notes: Added from Immunotherapy ZIP. Biomarker/translational paper from CA209-678 context.

publications.title: Segmental Yttrium-90 Radioembolization Using Glass Microspheres Greater than 400 Gray for the Treatment of Intrahepatic Cholangiocarcinoma: A Preliminary Experience
publications.authors: Yu et al.
publications.journal: Journal of Vascular and Interventional Radiology
publications.pdfFileName: metastases/ICC/2023 Yu Rad Seg using glass microspheres in ICC.pdf
publications.results: Study or review related to Y90 radioembolization for ICC liver metastases / non-HCC liver tumors. Details should be manually verified from the full text.
publications.findings:
• Radiation segmentectomy with a dose of >190 Gy using yttrium-90 (90Y) glass microspheres for intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (iCCA) has been shown to be safe and effective.
• The present study further increased the dose to >400 Gy for treatment of iCCA as complete pathologic necrosis has been shown in hepatocellular carcinoma using this ablative approach.
• A total of 10 patients with 13 tumors (median size, 5.3 cm; range, 1.5-13.6 cm) at a single institution underwent >400-Gy segmental radioembolization.
• Objective response was achieved in all tumors (13 of 13, 100%).
• One patient developed a Grade 3 or greater major adverse event (stroke and hepatic decompensation).
publications.notes: Added from metastases ZIP. Source file: metastases/ICC/2023 Yu Rad Seg using glass microspheres in ICC.pdf

publications.title: Voxel-Based Dosimetry Predicts Hepatotoxicity in Hepatocellular Carcinoma Patients Undergoing Radioembolization with 90Y Glass Microspheres
publications.authors: Watanabe M, Grafe H, Theysohn J, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: Simplicity/2023 Watanabe voxel based heaptotoxicity with Y90 glass microspheres.pdf
publications.results: Voxel-based dosimetry study evaluating prediction of hepatotoxicity in HCC patients undergoing Y90 glass microsphere radioembolization.
publications.findings:
• The area under the curve for prediction of posttherapeutic grade 3+ increases in bilirubin was acceptable for V20 (0.77), V30 (0.78), and V40 (0.79), whereas it was low for AD-WNTLT (0.67).
• The respective thresholds were 78% (V30), 72% (V40), and 43 Gy (AD-30).
• Voxel-based dosimetry may more accurately predict hepatotoxicity than multicompartment dosimetry in HCC patients undergoing radioembolization, which could enable dose escalation or deescalation with the intent to optimize treatment response.
publications.notes: Added from ROWAN ZIP. DOI 10.2967/jnumed.122.264996.

publications.title: Voxel-S-value methods adapted to heterogeneous media for quantitative Y-90 microsphere radioembolization dosimetry
publications.authors: Chen G, Lu Z, Chen Y, Mok GSP.
publications.pdfFileName: 2023 Chen Voxel value methods adapted to heterogeneous media for quantitative Y90 microsphere radioembolization dosimetry.pdf
publications.results: 10 patients analyzed; liver and tumor median absolute absorbed dose errors were <3% for all methods, while tissue-specific lung-kernel methods reduced lung-region errors to <7% versus >20% for methods without lung kernels.
publications.findings:
• Liver and tumor’s median %AADE of all methods were <3% for three types of imaging data.
• In the lungs, however, three recently proposed VSV methods provided median %AADEs of less than 7%, whereas the differences exceeded 20% for existing methods that did not use a lung kernel.
• LiCKLuKD could achieve median %AADE <2% in the liver, upper liver and tumors, and median %AADE <7% in the lungs and lower lungs in three types of data.
• Methods with tissue-specific kernel and effective correction achieve smaller errors in lungs.
• LiCKLuKD has comparable results with MC in absorbed dose estimation of Y-90 radioembolization for all target regions.
publications.notes: Technical dosimetry methodology paper; not a clinical outcomes study.

publications.title: 90Y Radioembolization versus Drug-eluting Bead Chemoembolization for Unresectable Hepatocellular Carcinoma: Results from the TRACE Phase II Randomized Controlled Trial
publications.authors: Dhondt E, Lambert B, Hermie L, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: TRACE/2022 Dhondt TRACEtrial.pdf
publications.results: Phase II randomized controlled TRACE trial comparing Y90 radioembolization with drug-eluting bead TACE in unresectable HCC.
publications.findings:
• Prospective uncontrolled studies suggest that yttrium 90 (90Y) transarterial radioembolization (TARE) is a safe and effective alternative.
• Purpose To compare the efficacy and safety of TARE with TACE for unresectable HCC.
• Materials and Methods In this single-center prospective randomized controlled trial (TRACE), 90Y glass TARE was compared with doxorubicin drug-eluting bead (DEB) TACE in participants with intermediate-stage HCC, extended to Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status 1 and those with early-stage HCC not eligible for surgery or thermoablation.
• The primary end point was time to overall tumor progression (TTP) (Kaplan-Meier analysis) in the intention-to-treat (ITT) and per-protocol (PP) groups.
• Results At interim analysis, 38 participants (median age, 67 years; IQR, 63-72 years; 33 men) were randomized to the TARE arm and 34 (median age, 68 years; IQR, 61-71 years; 30 men) to the DEB-TACE arm (ITT group).

publications.title: Clinical consensus statement: Selective internal radiation therapy with yttrium 90 resin microspheres for hepatocellular carcinoma in Asia
publications.authors: Liu DM, Leung TWT, Chow PKH, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2022 Liu Clinical consensus statement Selective internal radiation therapy with yttrium 90 resin microspheres for HCC in Asia.pdf
publications.results: Systematic literature review plus modified Delphi process with 10 Asian experts; provides Asia-specific recommendations and a patient selection algorithm for Y90 resin SIRT in HCC.
publications.findings:
• The experts acknowledged a general lack of evidence relating to use of SIRT in Asia and identified as an unmet need the lack of phase 3 randomized trials comparing clinical outcomes and survival following SIRT versus other therapies for HCC.
• Through an iterative process, the expert group explored areas of clinical relevance and generated 31 guidance statements and a patient management algorithm that achieved consensus.
• These recommendations aim to support clinicians in their decision-making and to help them identify and treat patients with HCC using SIRT in Asia.
• The recommendations also highlight areas in which further clinical trials are needed to define the role of SIRT in management of HCC among Asian populations.
publications.notes: Consensus statement for resin microspheres; keep product-specific language clear.

publications.title: First-in-man Histotripsy of Hepatic Tumors: The THERESA Trial, a Feasibility Study

publications.pdfFileName: First-in-man histotripsy of hepatic tumors  the THERESA trial  a feasibility study.pdf
publications.results: Prospective multicenter first-in-human feasibility trial. Eight eligible patients with 11 liver tumors were treated. Primary endpoint of acute technical success was achieved in all procedures, and no device-related adverse events were identified through 2 months.
publications.findings: Important first-in-human hepatic histotripsy study. It supports feasibility and early safety, while longer follow-up and larger studies are needed before making broader efficacy comparisons against established locoregional therapies.

publications.title: Hepatocellular carcinoma radiation segmentectomy treatment intensification prior to liver transplantation increases rates of complete pathologic necrosis: an explant analysis of 75 tumors
publications.authors: Montazeri SA, De la Garza-Ramos C, Lewis AR, et al.
publications.journal: European Journal of Nuclear Medicine and Molecular Imaging
publications.pdfFileName: RPM/2022 Montazeri HCC radiation segmentectomy treatment intensification prior to liver transplantation increases rates of complete pathologic necrosis an explant analysis of 75 tumors.pdf
publications.results: Retrospective single-center explant analysis of 75 HCC tumors treated with radiation segmentectomy prior to liver transplantation, comparing a treatment-intensification cohort with a baseline cohort.
publications.findings:
• Specific activity, dose, and treatment activity were significantly higher in the treatment intensification cohort (all p < 0.01), while particles per cubic centimeter of treated liver were not.
• ROC analysis showed that specific activity ≥ 327 Bq (AUC 0.75, p < 0.001), dose ≥ 446 Gy (AUC 0.69, p = 0.005), and treatment activity ≥ 2.55 Gbq (AUC 0.71, p = 0.002) were predictive of CPN.
• While dose strongly correlated with pathologic response, specific activity was the most significant independent radiation segmentectomy treatment parameter associated with CPN.
publications.notes: Added from ROWAN ZIP. DOI 10.1007/s00259-022-05776-y.

publications.authors: Kim HC, et al.

publications.results: Comparative analysis of long-term outcomes after TARE versus resection for large single HCC.
publications.findings:
• The resection group was younger (median, 60 vs.
• 69 y) and had a smaller tumor size (median, 7.0 vs.
• After baseline characteristics were balanced using inverse-probability-of-treatment weighting, the OS (hazard ratio [HR], 0.98; 95% CI, 0.40-2.43; P = 0.97), TTP (HR, 1.10; 95% CI, 0.55-2.20; P = 0.80), and TTIP (HR, 1.45; 95% CI, 0.72-2.93; P = 0.30) of the TARE group was comparable to the resection group.
• TARE was not an independent risk for OS (adjusted HR, 1.04; 95% CI, 0.42-2.59; P = 0.93), TTP (adjusted HR, 0.98; 95% CI, 0.50-1.95; P = 0.96), or TTIP (adjusted HR, 1.30; 95% CI, 0.65-2.58; P = 0.46).

publications.pdfFileName: Lung Shunt/2022 Stella Lung Dose Measured on Postradioembolization PETCT and Incidence of Radiation Pneumonitis.pdf
publications.results: 272 patients underwent Y-90 procedures; median predicted MAA lung dose was 3.5 Gy and median post-treatment Y-90 PET lung dose was 1 Gy. No patient had post-treatment lung dose >30 Gy; radiation pneumonitis occurred in patients with higher measured lung doses.

publications.title: Optimizing 90Y Particle Density Improves Outcomes After Radioembolization
publications.authors: Maxwell AWP, Mendoza HG, Sellitti MJ, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: 2022 Maxwell Optimizing 90Y particle density improves outcomes after radioembolization.pdf
publications.results: Evaluated the relationship between Y-90 particle density and outcomes after radioembolization.
publications.findings:
• Despite these large differences in dose and particle density, there was no difference in local progression-free survival or overall survival after resin versus glass microspheres (Figure 1).
• However, subgroup analysis (Supplemental Table 1) shows that hypervascular tumors (T:N ratio ≥ 3.3 on MAA scan) have better local progression free survival (LPFS) with glass microspheres, compared to resin (9 versus 2 months, p=0.003).
• There was a trend towards improved overall survival when hypervascular tumors were treated using glass microspheres (p=0.06), and a trend towards improved overall survival when less vascular tumors were treated using resin microspheres (p=0.08).
• There was no benefit to using resin microspheres for large tumor volumes, compared to glass, in terms of local progression free survival (p=0.17) or overall survival (p=0.54).
• Mean tumor dose did not predict local progression free survival (p=0.42) or overall survival (p=0.44) in our patient population (Table 3).

publications.title: Assessment of radiation sensitivity of unresectable intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma in a series of patients submitted to radioembolization with yttrium-90 resin microspheres
publications.authors: Depalo T, Traino AC, Bargellini I, et al.
publications.pdfFileName: Simplicity/Depalo 2021 ICC dosimetry y90 resin.pdf
publications.results: Series evaluating radiation sensitivity and dosimetric relationships in unresectable intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma treated with Y90 resin microspheres.
publications.findings:
• The essential implementation of the absorbed dose calculation methods should take into account also the specific tumor radiosensitivity, expressed by the α parameter.
• Tumor absorbed doses were calculated processing the post-therapeutic 90Y-PET/CT images and the pre-treatment contrast-enhanced CT scans.
• Tumor absorbed dose and pre- and post-treatment tumor volumes were used to calculate α and α3D parameters (dividing targeted liver in n voxels of the same volume with specific voxel absorbed dose).

• The median of α and α3D parameters was 0.005 Gy-1 (95% CI 0.004-0.008) and 0.007 Gy-1 (95% CI 0.005-0.015), respectively.

publications.title: Dose-Response and Dose-Toxicity Relationships for Glass 90Y Radioembolization in Patients with Liver Metastases from Colorectal Cancer
publications.authors: Alsultan et al.
publications.pdfFileName: metastases/mCRC/2021 Alsultan Dose-response and dose-toxicity relationships for Y90 glass RE in pts with mCRC.pdf
publications.results: Study or review related to Y90 radioembolization for mCRC liver metastases / non-HCC liver tumors. Details should be manually verified from the full text.
publications.findings:
• The median tumor-absorbed dose of 85 measurable metastases was 133 Gy (range, 20–1001 Gy).
• Per response category, this was 196 Gy for complete response (CR), 177 Gy for partial response (PR), 72 Gy for stable disease, and 95 Gy for progressive disease (PD).
• A significant dose–response relationship was found on a tumor level, with a significantly higher tumor-absorbed dose in metastases with CR (+94%) and PR (+74%) than in metastases with PD (P < 0.001).
• A similar relationship was found on a patient level, with PR having a higher tumor-absorbed dose than did PD (+58%, P = 0.044).
• A tumor-absorbed dose of more than 139 Gy predicted a 3-mo metabolic response with the greatest accuracy (89% specificity and 77% sensitivity), whereas a tumor-absorbed dose of more than 189 Gy predicted response with 97% specificity and 45% sensitivity.
publications.notes: Added from metastases ZIP. Source file: metastases/mCRC/2021 Alsultan Dose-response and dose-toxicity relationships for Y90 glass RE in pts with mCRC.pdf

publications.findings:
• The majority of patients with metastatic colorectal cancer (CRC) to the liver are not amenable to curative-intent surgery or thermal ablation; there is a need for alternative locoregional therapies to control oligometastatic liver disease.
